{"step_by_step_thinking": "The patient is a 27-year-old man with a 3-week history of left shoulder pain that worsens when sleeping on the affected side. He has tried pain medications without relief. On examination, there is severe tenderness at the greater tuberosity and bicipital groove, limited range of motion, and X-ray findings of narrowing of the acromiohumeral space with sclerosis and spurring of the acromion. The key additional finding is a positive rheumatoid factor in routine blood tests, which is a marker for autoimmune inflammation. While rotator cuff injuries and impingement syndromes are common in young patients with overhead activities (like tennis), they typically present with pain during overhead movements, a painful arc, and tenderness over the greater tuberosity, but do not usually show radiographic sclerosis and spurring of the acromion. Osteoarthritis is more common in older adults and typically presents with joint space narrowing and osteophytes, but it is less likely in a 27-year-old. Trauma is denied. Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune condition that can affect joints, leading to symmetric joint involvement, morning stiffness, and radiographic changes such as acromioclavicular spurring and sclerosis. The presence of a positive rheumatoid factor, along with joint tenderness, limited motion, and radiographic changes consistent with chronic inflammatory arthritis, strongly supports rheumatoid arthritis as the underlying cause. Although rotator cuff injury is common in this demographic, the presence of systemic autoimmunity markers (positive rheumatoid factor) and structural changes on X-ray point more toward a systemic inflammatory arthritis. Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is rheumatoid arthritis.", "answer_choice": "B"}
